hero_sub: Interactive exploration of major numerical computation methods. Visualize, compute and understand each algorithm step by step.
sec_roots_sub: Find the zeros of a function f(x) = 0
sec_integ_sub: Approximate the area under a curve
footer: TP Project — Numerical Analysis | Made with 🧠 and Python
error_fields: Please fill in all fields correctly.
error_signs: f(a) and f(b) must have opposite signs.
error_bounds: Bound a must be strictly less than b.
error_length: Lists x and y must have the same length.
dicho_desc: Bisection method: divide the interval in two at each iteration until converging to the root.
lagrange_desc: Build a polynomial passing exactly through a set of given points.
newton_desc: Divided differences interpolation, more efficient for adding new points.
simpson_desc: Integral approximation by parabolic arcs, very precise for regular functions.
trapeze_desc: Integral approximation by trapezoids, simple and efficient for regular intervals.
dicho_step2: If f(m) × f(a) > 0 → replace a with m
dicho_theory_p1: The bisection method finds a root of a continuous function f on an interval [a, b], provided that f(a) × f(b) < 0.
lagrange_subtitle: Build a polynomial passing through a set of given points
lagrange_theory_p1: Lagrange interpolation finds a unique polynomial of degree n-1 passing exactly through n given points.
lagrange_step1: For each point i, compute the basis polynomial Li(x)
lagrange_step2: Multiply by the corresponding value yi
lagrange_step3: Sum all terms to get P(x)
newton_theory_p1: Newton interpolation uses divided differences to build a polynomial passing through n points.
newton_step2: Evaluate the polynomial at the requested point
simpson_theory_p1: Simpson's method approximates the integral by subdividing the interval into 2m sub-intervals and approximating the function by parabolic arcs.
trapeze_theory_p1: The trapezoidal method approximates the integral by subdividing the interval into m sub-intervals and approximating the function by linear segments.
gauss_subtitle: Solving linear systems by row echelon form and back substitution
gauss_theory_p1: Gaussian elimination transforms the augmented matrix [A|b] into upper triangular form, then solves by back substitution.
gauss_step1: Phase 1 — Elimination: For each pivot k, eliminate column k below the diagonal
gauss_step3: Phase 2 — Back substitution: Solve bottom-up x[i] = (b[i] − Σ A[i,j]·x[j]) / A[i,i]
gaussjordan_subtitle: Reduced row echelon form — the solution appears directly in the last column
gaussjordan_theory_p1: Gauss-Jordan extends Gaussian elimination by continuing to eliminate above each pivot, producing an identity matrix on the left. The solution X is read directly from the last column.
gaussjordan_step1: Normalize each pivot row: M[i,:] = M[i,:] / M[i,i]
gaussjordan_step2: Eliminate in all other rows (above and below)
gaussjordan_step3: Result: [I | X] — no need for back substitution!
lu_subtitle: Factorization of A into L (lower) and U (upper) to solve Ax = b
lu_theory_p1: LU decomposition factors a matrix A into A = L × U, which greatly simplifies solving Ax = b into two triangular systems.
lu_step1: Factorization: Find L and U such that A = LU
lu_step2: Forward substitution: Solve Ly = b for y
lu_step3: Back substitution: Solve Ux = y for x
cholesky_subtitle: Factorization A = RᵀR for symmetric positive definite matrices
cholesky_theory_p1: The Cholesky method is an optimized LU factorization (twice as fast) exclusively for symmetric positive definite matrices.
cholesky_step1: Factorization: Find an upper triangular matrix R such that A = RᵀR
pointfixe_subtitle: Find x such that g(x) = x by successive iterations
pointfixe_theory_p1: The fixed point method transforms the equation f(x) = 0 into x = g(x) and iterates from an initial guess x0.
sec_linear_sub: Solve systems of equations Ax = b
dicho_card_desc: Bisection method: divide the interval in two at each iteration until converging to the root.
pointfixe_card_desc: Find x* such that g(x*) = x* by successive iterations xₙ₊₁ = g(xₙ) until convergence.
lagrange_card_desc: Build a polynomial passing exactly through a set of given points using basis polynomials Li(x).
newton_card_desc: Interpolation by divided differences, more efficient for adding new points progressively.
simpson_card_desc: Integral approximation by parabolic arcs, very precise for smooth functions.
trapeze_card_desc: Integral approximation by trapezoids, simple and efficient for regular intervals.
gauss_card_desc: Solving linear systems by row echelon form and back substitution.
gaussjordan_card_desc: Reduced row echelon form — the solution appears directly in the last column.
lu_card_desc: Factorization of A into L (lower) and U (upper) to solve Ax = b.
cholesky_card_desc: Factorization A = RᵀR for symmetric positive definite matrices.
pointfixe_desc: Example: to solve x² - x - 2 = 0, enter g(x) = sqrt(x+2) or g(x) = 1 + 2/x
pointfixe_interactive_desc: Enter the iteration function g(x) such that g(x*) = x*. Example: for x² - x - 2 = 0, enter sqrt(x+2) or 1 + 2/x
